summary: Bryan Cantrill announces Samsung's acquisition of Joyent, explaining how cloud computing shifted innovation from hardware designers to problem-solvers, leaving legacy computing companies unable to compete. He contrasts the fearful, conflicted approaches of traditional infrastructure companies with Samsung's genuine technical engagement and shared values around robustness and understanding system behavior. The acquisition represents not an 'exit' but an 'entrance' to new scale, with Samsung committed to keeping Joyent's platform open source.
key_points: Cloud computing shifted innovation from machine designers to those solving real problems
Legacy computing companies fear rather than embrace cloud-native technology
Samsung proved serious by providing hardware for scale testing when Joyent couldn't
Shared values around robustness and debugging were key to the partnership
Open source commitment was non-negotiable for Joyent
money_quotes: Cloud computing managed to achieve what previous computing revolutions did not: shift the wellspring of innovation away from those who merely design machines towards those who actually use them to solve their own problems.

These legacy companies have never themselves meaningfully deployed on the cloud and (despite nodding along with cloud computing) they still really struggle to understand why anyone would do it that way.
Their worst nightmare was that our technology would thrive under their aegis and pose a mortal threat to their existing (and more comfortable) lines of business.
Having seen first-hand not only the technical but also the commercial benefits of being open source—there was no way we would contemplate returning to the dark ages of proprietary software!
The acquisition of Joyent by Samsung is not—in Silicon Valley's tacky vernacular—an 'exit', but rather an entrance.
key_insight: The cloud computing revolution succeeded where previous computing shifts failed by transferring power from hardware designers to operators who solve real problems, leaving legacy companies paralyzed by fear of disrupting their own business models.
